topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun cast long, thin shadows across my porch as I watched Mr. Henderson meticulously measure the lengths of wood. Each precise cut he made seemed to echo the hollow ache in my chest. I kept tracing patterns on the dusty railing with my fingertip, the wood rough beneath my touch. He kept glancing over, a kind, oblivious smile playing on his lips, completely unaware of the quiet weight pressing down on me. I just nodded, a small, jerky movement, trying to appear engaged while my gaze drifted to the overgrown patch of weeds by my mailbox. It felt like watching a slow, inevitable erosion of everything familiar.

[story 2]

I stood by the window, the glass cool against my forehead, watching the construction crew begin to stake out the boundary line. The sound of the hammering was sharp, insistent, slicing through the stillness of the evening. My shoulders slumped inward, a heavy, defeated curve. I picked at a loose thread on my sleeve, my eyes fixed on the distant line of the new fence, a stark, dividing barrier between us. "It's going to look so neat," I mumbled to myself, the words barely audible, thick with an unspoken resignation. I didn't move to join the conversation happening near the driveway; I just stood there, rooted to the spot, feeling utterly drained.

[story 3]

When Mrs. Gable brought over the blueprints, the sheer scale of the project made my breath catch. I stared at the diagrams, the lines and measurements swimming before my eyes, and a strange numbness settled over me. I fiddled constantly with the edge of my worn cardigan, unable to meet her gaze when she spoke about the cost and the timeline. Every word felt like sand slipping through my fingers. I managed a thin, strained smile, the muscles around my mouth refusing to lift properly, and I focused intensely on a small crack in the pavement, wishing the ground would simply swallow me whole.
